title: California Federal Judge Blocks ICE Courthouse Arrests Nationwide, Cites 'Complete Lack of Decision-Making'
excerpt: U.S. District Judge P. Casey Pitts vacated the Trump administration's courthouse-arrest policies entirely on a nationwide basis, finding them 'arbitrary and capricious' under the Administrative Procedure Act and describing ICE's 2025 policy reversal as reflecting a 'complete lack of decision-making.'
content:
On June 24, 2026, U.S. District Judge P. Casey Pitts of the Northern District of California issued a nationwide ruling vacating the Trump administration's courthouse-arrest policies in Pablo Sequen v. Albarran.

  • The 71-page decision found that ICE and the Executive Office for Immigration Review had adopted the 2025 courthouse-arrest expansion in an 'arbitrary and capricious' manner, violating the Administrative Procedure Act's requirement for reasoned decision-making.
  • Judge Pitts wrote that the government demonstrated a 'complete lack of decision-making' in adopting new policies — and that ICE never addressed the 'chilling effect' that courthouse arrests had on immigrant attendance at mandatory hearings, even though that concern was central to the earlier guidance it was abandoning.
  • The ruling struck down: (1) two ICE directives authorizing civil arrests at immigration courthouses, and (2) an EOIR policy extending short-term detention from 12 to 72 hours.
  • The order reinstated pre-2025 limits: courthouse arrests permitted only for national security threats, direct pursuit, or where arrest elsewhere was impossible.

The ruling went significantly further than earlier regional injunctions and applied nationwide, finding that officers had been deployed under guidance that ICE itself could not legally defend — a further documented failure to provide agents with lawful, coherent arrest authority standards.
mediaPlaceholder: 71-page ruling by Judge Pitts with highlighted sections on arbitrary and capricious ICE decision-making
imageAlt: California Federal Judge Blocks ICE Courthouse Arrests Nationwide, Cites 'Complete Lack of Decision-Making'
